summary: Scottish poet. First female (and first openly LGBT) British Poet Laureate (2009-2019), succeeded by Simon Armitage. Known for The World's Wife (dramatic monologues from perspectives of famous men's female counterparts), Standing Female Nude, and the T.S. Eliot Prize-winning Rapture.

works.name: The World's Wife
works.description: Collection of dramatic monologues told from the perspectives of female counterparts to famous male figures.

clues.clue: Poems from perspectives of famous men's female counterparts (e.g., Mrs. Tiresias, Anne Hathaway, Queen Herod, Elvis's Twin Sister)
examples: poems about female counterparts of famous men such as Charles Darwin and Pontius Pilate

clues.clue: "Anne Hathaway" — Shakespeare's wife holds "him in the casket of my widow's head"; marriage bed likened to "seas where he would dive for pearls"

a character lets a wolf buy her a drink and then kills him with an ax 10 years later

clues.clue: "Elvis's Twin Sister" — a nun thinks of her convent as "a land of grace"; ends with reference to Heartbreak Hotel
examples: A nun thinks of her convent as 'a land of grace'... 'a long time since I walked down Lonely Street / towards Heartbreak Hotel'

comprehensive_summary: Carol Ann Duffy is clued as a Scottish poet who served as British Poet Laureate from 2009 to 2019, the first woman, first Scot, and first openly LGBT person to hold the position. She was succeeded by Simon Armitage. Her most frequently clued work is The World's Wife, a collection of dramatic monologues from the perspectives of female counterparts to famous men. Key poems from this collection include "Anne Hathaway" (about Shakespeare's wife reflecting on the "second best bed" she was bequeathed), "Little Red-Cap" (a retelling where the speaker kills a wolf with an axe for the sake of "poetry"), "Elvis's Twin Sister" (a nun in a convent thinking of Heartbreak Hotel), "Mrs. Tiresias" (whose husband went out a man and came home female), and "Queen Herod" (with three female Magi). Her first collection Standing Female Nude includes the title poem written from the perspective of an artist's model. "Education for Leisure" was censored in schools for knife-crime references. "Valentine" offers an onion as a love token. "Last Post" inverts Wilfred Owen's "Dulce et Decorum Est" to eulogize WWI veterans. Rapture won the T.S. Eliot Prize. She also wrote political poetry including "Campaign" about Brexit and "The Assassination of Margaret Thatcher" adaptation of Everyman. She was a member of the "New Generation" school succeeding the Martian poets and had a decade-long relationship with Liverpool Poet Adrian Henri.
